scriptFull:
HOW THEY GOT IN.
The technical entry point for Salt Typhoon was not sophisticated in the way movies portray hacking. There were no dramatic countdown timers, no Hollywood moments of watching code scroll by while racing against a clock.
What there was, was patience, and the systematic exploitation of vulnerabilities that had existed for years — some of them patched, but never applied.
Salt Typhoon's primary entry vector, documented by Trend Micro, Bleeping Computer, and multiple congressional briefings, was the exploitation of known vulnerabilities in edge devices — the equipment that sits at the boundary of a corporate network and the outside world. Routers, firewalls, VPN gateways. Specifically, they repeatedly targeted Ivanti Connect Secure VPN systems, Fortinet FortiGate appliances, and Cisco routers.
These were not zero-day exploits — meaning vulnerabilities unknown to the vendor — but rather what researchers call N-day exploits: flaws that had been publicly disclosed, flaws for which patches had already been issued and distributed, and flaws that multiple congressional aides confirmed the targeted telecom companies had simply never bothered to apply. In some cases, the vulnerabilities dated back to 2018, meaning that six years of available remediation had been ignored entirely. In one particularly striking documented case, a management system protecting critical network infrastructure was secured with nothing more than a basic numeric password — the kind of credential that every basic security guide has warned against for decades.
Once inside the edge device, Salt Typhoon moved with a discipline that made them extraordinarily difficult to detect. Their core technique is what the industry calls Living off the Land — using the network's own legitimate administrative tools to move through it, rather than importing malware that security software might flag. They issued commands through Cisco's native interfaces. They used Windows Management Instrumentation. They leveraged PowerShell. To a network monitoring tool, their activity looked indistinguishable from a system administrator doing routine maintenance.
They then deployed a rootkit called Demodex — a kernel-level implant that conceals its own presence from the operating system and employs anti-forensic techniques to survive investigation. And they installed a modular backdoor called GhostSpider: a tool that lives entirely in memory, leaves no files on disk, and hides its command-and-control traffic inside normal HTTP headers. Even if someone was looking directly at the network traffic, GhostSpider was designed to look like routine web browsing.
In at least one documented environment, Salt Typhoon maintained undetected access for three years.

scenes.prompt: Close-up, photorealistic shot of a dusty, abandoned office desk with a sticky note on a monitor screen. The note has a simple numeric code written on it. Dim, moody lighting, cinematic focus.

content.BODY_TEXT: IMPLANT SURVIVES REBOOT AND MEMORY DUMP

scenes.script: And they installed a modular backdoor called GhostSpider: a tool that lives entirely in memory, leaves no files on disk,
and hides its command-and-control traffic inside normal HTTP headers. Even if someone was looking directly at the network traffic, GhostSpider was designed to look like routine web browsing.
In at least one documented environment, Salt Typhoon maintained undetected access for three years.
